name: Modern Landscape
environment: The contemporary surface of Emery County, carved by rivers, wind, and water over millennia. Soils, vegetation, and modern deposits blanket older formations.
fossils: Modern flora and fauna, archaeological artifacts
economic: Agricultural lands, mineral extraction, tourism destinations

name: Colob Conglomerate
environment: Fluvial deposits from uplift and erosion. Coarse debris flows and stream gravels shed from rising highlands during the Laramide orogeny. Represents rapid erosion and transport of older rocks.
fossils: Sparse vertebrate remains, trace fossils

name: North Horn Formation
environment: Transitional zone between marine and non-marine environments. Fluvial and lacustrine deposits. Marks the regression of the Western Interior Seaway as the Cretaceous ended.
fossils: Dinosaur bones, freshwater gastropods, charophytes
economic: Coal layers interbedded with other strata

name: Price River Formation
environment: Fluvial delta and stream deposits. Sands, silts, and muds from a complex river system draining the western Sevier highlands. Represents a major transition in depositional setting.
fossils: Dinosaur trace fossils, plant fragments, freshwater mollusks
economic: Important coal seams; subject of intense mining history

name: Castlegate Sandstone
environment: Nearshore and deltaic sandstones representing a broad, shifting delta. Fine to coarse-grained, well-sorted sands laid down where rivers met the seaway.
fossils: Trace fossils, rare dinosaur remains, marine invertebrates

name: Blackhawk Formation
environment: Upper shoreface and delta-front deposits. Represents submarine fans and sand bars at the margin of the Western Interior Seaway during a major regression.
fossils: Marine invertebrates, ammonites, inoceramids, trace fossils
economic: Coal seams; part of the coal-bearing interval

name: Mancos Shale
environment: Deep marine basin. Fine-grained muds and silts deposited far from shore during a major highstand of the Western Interior Seaway. Rich in organic material.
fossils: Abundant ammonites, inoceramids, fish teeth, marine reptile bones, foraminifera
economic: Oil and gas source rock; bentonite beds

name: Cedar Mountain Formation
environment: Fluvial and lacustrine deposits. Rivers and lakes in a complex pre-seaway landscape. Important transition zone marking the onset of marine transgression.
fossils: Dinosaur bones and teeth, fresh-water gastropods, plant remains

name: Morrison Formation
environment: Fluvial and lacustrine settings. Meandering rivers and ephemeral lakes with muddy floodplains. Semi-arid continental environment. World-famous for dinosaur fossils.
fossils: Abundant dinosaur bones (Allosaurus, Brachiosaurus, Diplodocus), turtles, crocodilians, fish, plants

name: Summerville Formation
environment: Transitional fluvial to marine environment. Mudstones and siltstones with ripple marks and cross-bedding. Mixed continental and marine influences.
fossils: Marine reptiles, fish, sparse dinosaur remains, marine invertebrates

name: Curtis Formation
environment: Marine sandstones and siltstones. Shallow sea deposits with evidence of storms and wave action. Transitional from non-marine to fully marine.
fossils: Marine invertebrates, belemnites, ammonites, marine reptiles

name: Entrada Sandstone
environment: Coastal and nearshore deposits. Clean, well-sorted sandstones with cross-bedding and ripples. Represents a shallow marine environment transitioning from arid flats.
fossils: Sparse trace fossils, rare marine invertebrates

name: Carmel Formation
environment: Shallow marine deposits with significant evaporitic character. Evidence of restricted basins, tidal flats, and saline lagoons.
fossils: Marine invertebrates, rare ammonites, foraminifera

name: Navajo Sandstone
environment: Eolian dunes and interdune deposits. Vast desert with shifting dunes, shallow lakes, and braided streams. Represents the Jurassic Sahara of North America.
fossils: Trace fossils, rare vertebrate remains, wind-blown plant material
economic: Building stone; aquifer; some uranium and vanadium mineralization

name: Kayenta Formation
environment: Fluvial and lacustrine deposits. Meandering streams, shallow lakes, and muddy floodplains. Transitional between the Wingate desert and Navajo dunes.
fossils: Dinosaur bones and teeth, crocodile remains, turtles, fish, plants

name: Wingate Sandstone
environment: Large-scale eolian dunes with interdune lakes and streams. High-energy desert environment with massive cross-bedding. Distinctive rose-colored cliffs.
fossils: Trace fossils, rare vertebrate remains, petrified logs

name: Chinle Formation
environment: Fluvial and lacustrine deposits on a broad floodplain. Multi-colored muds, silts, and sands with evidence of channels, lakes, and ephemeral streams.
fossils: Dinosaurs, phytosaurs, aetosaurs, fish, ferns, conifers, petrified logs
economic: Uranium and vanadium mineralization historically significant

name: Moenkopi Formation
environment: Shallow marine and supratidal deposits. Tidal flats, lagoons, and near-shore muds with ripple marks and desiccation cracks. Heavily influenced by seasonal floods.
fossils: Trace fossils, rare marine and freshwater invertebrates, plant fragments

name: Cutler Formation
environment: Fluvial and deltaic deposits with fan alluvium. Red beds shed from rising highlands. Complex braided streams, mudflats, and sparse vegetation.
fossils: Plant remains, trace fossils, rare arthropods, early reptiles
economic: Uranium and vanadium potential; some mineral resources

name: Hermosa Group
environment: Shallow marine and nearshore deposits. Cyclic alternation of limestone, shale, and sandstone reflecting changes in sea level. Carboniferous tropical seas.
fossils: Abundant marine invertebrates, crinoids, brachiopods, foraminifera, fish
economic: Petroleum source rock; minor gas production historically

name: Paradox Formation
environment: Restricted marine basin with cyclic evaporites, salt, and shales. Periodic connection to open ocean alternating with closed-basin conditions. Hypersaline lagoons.
fossils: Foraminifera, ostracods, rare marine invertebrates in non-evaporitic intervals
economic: Salt and potash deposits; source of oil and gas

name: Leadville Limestone
environment: Shallow marine carbonate platform. Clear, warm tropical seas. Crinoidal limestones and dolomites. Represents peak transgression of Mississippian seas.
fossils: Abundant crinoids, brachiopods, trilobites, corals, foraminifera

name: Ouray Formation
environment: Shallow marine limestone and dolomite. Coral and stromatoporoid reefs with associated lime mud. Warm, clear, shallow tropical seas.
fossils: Coral heads, stromatoporoids, brachiopods, trilobites, fish remains

name: Elbert Formation
environment: Basal marine transgression. Shallow marine sandstones, siltstones, and limestones marking the beginning of Devonian seas. Represents coastal settings.
fossils: Early fish, marine invertebrates, scattered fossils

name: Aneth Formation
environment: Marine carbonate and clastic deposits. Shallow-water limestones and dolomites with interbedded siliciclastics. Represents marine transgression and regression cycles.
fossils: Trilobites, brachiopods, corals, crinoids, conodonts
economic: Oil and gas source rock potential

name: Viking Formation
environment: Marine sandstones and siltstones. Shallow-water clastic deposits in a transgressive sea. Fine-grained sediments with ripple marks and trace fossils.
fossils: Trilobites, brachiopods, ostracods, conodonts, graptolites

name: Fisset Brook Formation
environment: Marginal marine deposits. Nearshore sandstones and siltstones with evidence of shallow-water environments and coastal processes.
fossils: Trace fossils, rare body fossils

name: Pogonip Group
environment: Shallow marine carbonate platform. Limestone and dolomite with evidence of tropical seas. Crinoidal and oolitic limestones.
fossils: Trilobites, brachiopods, crinoids, corals, nautiloids, conodonts

name: Sentiment Mountain Formation
environment: Shallow marine clastic deposits. Sandstones and siltstones with wave ripples and cross-bedding. Clean sands deposited in high-energy nearshore environments.
fossils: Trace fossils, early trilobites, archaeocyathids, brachiopods

name: Camelback Mountain Formation
environment: Shallow marine marine deposits with mixed lithology. Siltstones, mudstones, and fine sandstones deposited in a subtidal environment.
fossils: Early trilobites, arthropods, primitive shells, trace fossils

name: Tintic Quartzite
environment: Mature quartz sands on a stable continental shelf. Well-sorted, cross-bedded sandstones. Represents a long period of shallow marine sedimentation.
fossils: Trace fossils, rare body fossils, matted algae

name: Precambrian Basement
environment: Ancient metamorphic and granitic rocks. Multiple cycles of mountain building, erosion, and deposition. The deep foundation of the continent.
fossils: Rare metamorphic minerals and ancient carbonaceous traces
